narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 72.3% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales against admission. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90—which falls below the 3.24 average among those who passed the bar—along with your LSAT score, family income in the middle bracket, and plans to attend full-time. The three biggest factors driving this outcome were your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, your LSAT performance, and your Year 1 decile of 2; these stood out as having the strongest sway over the model's decision for you specifically. Most critically, your low Year 3 decile—far below the typical 5.91 decile for bar passers—pushed the prediction heavily toward expecting failure, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you at a notable disadvantage compared to successful peers. In contrast, your LSAT score of 39, which exceeds the 37.27 average of those who passed, worked strongly in your favor, nudging the outlook toward passing and highlighting a key strength in your profile. Your Year 1 decile of 2, while better than your later performance, still pulled toward a failure prediction since it lags well behind the 6.04 average for passers, underscoring a pattern of grades that didn't keep pace with top performers. Overall, your situation differs from typical accepted students, who often cluster in higher deciles and show more consistent academic climbs, even though strengths like your LSAT and middle-income background align with many who succeed. Being full-time, like 93% of bar passers, is a plus but couldn't offset the decile concerns here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to build on your solid score or bolstering your GPA through post-baccalaureate courses or targeted academic programs that could lift your decile rankings in future evaluations. Many students in similar positions have improved their profiles by seeking mentorship from law advisors or enrolling in prep courses proven to correlate with bar success. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors at schools like ours. You're clearly motivated, and with focused steps, your path to law school remains very achievable.
